original_abstract:
This volume offers a comprehensive analysis of the transformative pressures reshaping higher education on a global scale. As universities move from elite to mass and market-oriented systems, they face the dual challenge of maintaining local relevance while pursuing international connectivity. Through a diverse range of regional case studies—spanning Europe, East Asia, and North America—the contributors examine how shifting welfare state dynamics, new managerialism, and the rise of private providers are altering the mission of public institutions.

Central to the discussion is the precarious state of academic freedom and the erosion of traditional collegial governance in favor of corporate-style performance metrics. Furthermore, the collection investigates the evolving relationship between higher education and lifelong learning, addressing how demographic shifts and technological disruptions, such as MOOCs, are unbundling traditional degree structures. By exploring the tension between expansion and equity, this work provides critical insights into whether contemporary reforms are successfully democratizing knowledge or merely reinforcing existing social stratifications in a rapidly changing world.
humanized_abstract:
This book tackles the challenge of change and mapping the changing landscape of Higher Education (HE) world-wide. HE is shifting from being elite or even mass higher education systems to becoming market-oriented systems which have to balance different forms of accountability towards the host country/region on the one hand and towards an increasingly globalising environment on the other. With empirics ranging from Europe over East Asia to North America, the contributions within this volume show how these shifts in welfare state models, together with the increasing impact of new public management and private higher education on a global scale, affect the core business of public higher education.

The contributions in this volume all pertain in one way or another to central issues of our time for higher education, including academic freedom and the regulation of academic life, the proper scope for collegial governance, the relationship between higher education and lifelong learning, the ongoing processes of differentiation and selectivity in the context of increasing numbers of students and the coming of MOOCs, the potential for increased social equity and access to knowledge through new arrangements and programs, and the ways in which all of these developments affect the very idea of a university in the context of a world undergoing profound changes. Collectively, the contributions highlight the challenges facing the ideal of learning as a liberal activity and therefore as an end in itself, rather than as a means to an economic or career-related end. Common to the contributions is an effort to illuminate the consequences of new developments, and to open a discussion regarding the degree to which these developments are compatible with the basic ideas of the university, including academic freedom, a comprehensive approach to knowledge, a focus on learning as an end in itself, the promotion of responsible and autonomous individuals, and a spirit of solidarity and mutual respect among educators and students.
